retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with rheumatic heart disease are best managed by an interprofessional team. Primary care clinicians who have patients with a known diagnosis of rheumatic fever should maintain and stress the importance of strict follow-up with the patients. To prevent rheumatic heart disease, patients should undergo close monitoring with complete history and physicals and transthoracic echocardiography. Patients should be referred to cardiology if they start developing a new murmur, signs, and symptoms of heart failure, arrhythmia, or evidence of valvular disease on transthoracic echocardiography. Surgery is often required when the mitral and/or aortic valve is severely damaged.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the aberrant immune response to Streptococcus pyogenes infection in the pathophysiology of rheumatic heart disease. Outline the patient history associated with rhematic heart disease. Summarize the echocardiographic criteria for the diagnosis of rheumatic heart disease. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to stress the importance of strict follow-up to improve outcomes in those with rheumatic heart disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart disease is highly prevalent in developing countries leading to the cause of the most cardiovascular morbidity and mortality in young people. Guidelines provided by the World Health Federation have outlined that the most cost-effective way to prevent rheumatic heart disease is with antibiotic prophylaxis after the diagnosis of rheumatic fever. Patients should be informed of their diagnosis of rheumatic fever as well as the complications associated with poor compliance with antibiotics and follow-up including valvular heart disease, heart failure, and arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart disease is a systemic immune condition that occurs as a complication of rheumatic fever. This occurs after a beta-hemolytic streptococcal infection of the throat. Rheumatic heart disease is a critical form of acquired heart disease in children and adults worldwide. This activity illustrates the evaluation and management of rheumatic heart disease and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Nurses and pharmacists contribute significantly to patient education, ensuring adherence to antibiotic therapy and reinforcing the importance of continuous prophylaxis. Strong interprofessional communication and care coordination are essential for seamless transitions between acute management and long-term follow-up, minimizing recurrence and disease progression. By working collaboratively, healthcare professionals enhance team performance, reduce morbidity, and improve patient outcomes, ultimately preventing complications associated with ARF and RHD.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective management of ARF requires a coordinated, interprofessional team approach to optimize patient-centered care, safety, and outcomes. Primary care, urgent, and emergency care clinicians are often the first to evaluate patients with ARF, making early recognition of symptoms crucial. Prompt initiation of pharmacotherapy, including penicillin G for secondary prophylaxis and early cardiac assessment, is essential in reducing complications. If carditis or RHD is detected, cardiologists and cardiothoracic surgeons should lead the management of heart failure and valvular disease. Infectious disease specialists play a key role in guiding initial antibiotic treatment and long-term prophylaxis to prevent recurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical features of acute rheumatic fever. Select recommended diagnostic studies to evaluate patients with suspected acute rheumatic fever. Implement the appropriate management approach for acute rheumatic fever. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes for patients with acute rheumatic fever. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Care for patients with ARF should be carried out in consultation with an infectious disease specialist and a cardiologist. As part of the diagnostic criteria, it will be necessary to obtain echocardiography to look for evidence of carditis. If patients develop carditis with severe valvulopathy with signs of heart failure, a cardiothoracic surgeon should be consulted to consider valvuloplasty. In patients with Sydenham chorea, consultation with a neurologist may be necessary. In patients with persistent arthralgias and arthritis, consultation with a rheumatologist may help design a long-term management plan.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart disease results from either a single or repeated attacks of rheumatic fever that results in rigidity and deformity of valve cusps, the fusion of the commissures, or shortening and fusion of the chordae tendineae. Over 2 to 3 decades, valvular stenosis and/or regurgitation results. In chronic rheumatic heart disease, the mitral valve alone is the most commonly affected valve in an estimated 50% to 60% of cases. Combined lesions of both the aortic and mitral valves occur in 20% of cases. Involvement of the tricuspid valve occurs in about 10% of cases but only in association with mitral or aortic disease. Tricuspid valve cases are thought to be more common when recurrent infections have occurred. The pulmonary valve is rarely affected.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart disease is a systemic immune process that is sequelae to a beta-hemolytic streptococcal infection of the pharynx. It is most common in developing countries. It is responsible for 250,000 deaths in young people worldwide each year. Over 15 million people have evidence of rheumatic heart disease. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever is the primary cause of acquired heart disease in children and young adults worldwide. Rheumatic fever occurs 2 to 3 weeks after a group A beta-hemolytic streptococcal pharyngeal infection. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharyngitis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients and parents should be educated on the differences between bacterial and viral pharyngitis. Patients diagnosed with group A streptococcus infection should be encouraged to complete a full course of antibiotics to prevent the risk of rheumatic heart disease. Patients with viral pharyngitis should be instructed to treat the symptoms with OTC pain relievers and that there is no need for antibiotics. This will help to decrease antibiotic overprescribing and bacterial drug resistance. All patients with pharyngitis should be educated on the importance of handwashing, rest, and hydration.

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Stenosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral stenosis is a relatively common disorder, which, if left untreated, is associated with high morbidity and mortality. Mitral stenosis rates had decreased in the US four decades ago, but with the mass migration of individuals from other countries, a resurgence of cases has been observed. The number one cause for most cases of mitral stenosis is rheumatic fever. The key is to prevent the valvular disorder by ensuring that patients with strep throat are adequately treated. Because of the high morbidity of the disorder, the condition is best managed by an interprofessional team.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Manifestations of Metabolic Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Shared decision-making emerges as a pivotal component of this collaborative effort, empowering patients to participate actively in their care journey. Informed by thorough discussions and mutual understanding, decisions regarding diagnostic tests, treatment options, and long-term management must align with patients' preferences, values, and goals. Effective communication acts as the glue that binds this interprofessional team together, facilitating the exchange of insights, resolution of uncertainties, and care coordination. Through regular consultations, interprofessional meetings, and shared electronic health records, healthcare professionals ensure that no aspect of the patient's condition is overlooked and interventions are seamlessly integrated across specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: ARF and the subsequent development of RHD are significant causes of morbidity and mortality, especially in developing countries. Deterrence involves strategies to avoid GAS pharyngitis, early detection and treatment of GAS pharyngitis, and prophylaxis against recurrent ARF. Patient education is one of the cornerstones of successful lifelong management of ARF. Patients should be informed about the consequences of their diagnosis of ARF and the importance of adherence to prophylactic antibiotic therapy. Caregivers should also be educated about the signs of GAS pharyngitis and early manifestations of ARF. Household and school-aged contacts are the most likely sources of GAS exposure in people with a history of ARF.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2866
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive heart failure; nonhypertensive is identified as a manifestation of مرض قلبي روماتزمي مزمن (chronic rheumatic heart disease) and its development is attributed to الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية (rheumatic fever). This relationship underscores that rheumatic fever can lead to a non‑hypertensive form of congestive heart failure, which is classified within the spectrum of chronic rheumatic cardiac disease, informing both diagnostic considerations and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with the need for aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي). The inflammatory damage caused by rheumatic fever can affect heart valves, leading to severe dysfunction of the aortic and coronary valves that may require surgical replacement. Recognizing this relationship enables clinicians to monitor patients with rheumatic fever for progressive valvular disease and to plan timely surgical intervention, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879
retrieved_docs.content: Supraventricular tachycardia, a rapid heart rhythm originating above the ventricles, is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease, a valvular disorder that results from rheumatic fever. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating patients with supraventricular tachycardia for underlying rheumatic heart disease, as the presence of rheumatic valve damage can influence both the presentation and management strategies for the arrhythmia. Recognizing this association aids clinicians in providing comprehensive cardiac care and tailoring treatment plans to address both the rhythm disturbance and the structural heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1149
retrieved_docs.content: whereas services that omit continuous ECG monitoring are excluded from protocols that require Holter monitoring. Together, these entities and relationships illustrate an integrated approach to the diagnosis, monitoring, treatment, and rehabilitation of cardiovascular conditions, particularly those arising from rheumatic fever‑related valvular disease and other cardiac pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1317
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic cardiac involvement that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when evaluating patients with rheumatic heart disease, as it helps differentiate these acute forms from more chronic rheumatic cardiac conditions and guides timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: is linked to loss of biological function (Failure) and may occur concurrently with the rapid cardiac inflammation of acute rheumatic conditions, underscoring the potential for extra‑cardiac organ dysfunction. Overall, the knowledge base depicts rheumatic fever and its cardiac sequelae as a tightly connected group of entities—ranging from valve‑specific failures (particularly mitral and tricuspid) to diffuse myocardial and pericardial inflammation—typically presenting abruptly and briefly, with possible simultaneous hepatic and renal compromise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: Heart valve disorders are classified as chronic rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي مزمن), indicating that these valvular abnormalities fall within the spectrum of rheumatic heart disease that persists over time and requires appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic attention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: rheumatic inflammation, chronic valvular pathology, electrophysiologic support, coronary artery disease, and systemic complications intersect to inform comprehensive diagnosis, surgical planning, and patient management in rheumatic heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2780
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is identified as a cause of non‑hypertensive congestive heart failure (congestive heart failure; nonhypertensive). This form of congestive heart failure is classified as a subtype of rheumatic heart disease, other unspecified (مرض قلبي روماتزمي، غير ذلك وغير محدد). Consequently, rheumatic fever can lead to rheumatic heart disease, which may manifest clinically as non‑hypertensive congestive heart failure, highlighting the pathogenic link between the infectious trigger, the underlying cardiac disease category, and its specific functional presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1657
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is linked to an unspecified circulatory system disorder (اضطراب في جهاز الدورة الدموية غير محدد), which is considered a subcategory of the disease. The same unspecified circulatory disorder is also a child of chronic rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي مزمن). Congestive heart failure, non‑hypertensive type (congestive heart failure; nonhypertensive) is classified as a form of chronic rheumatic heart disease and is placed under the broader category of rheumatic heart disease, other and unspecified (مرض قلبي روماتزمي، غير ذلك وغير محدد). Although one source lists congestive heart failure, non‑hypertensive as a cause of rheumatic fever, the prevailing medical understanding is that congestive heart failure is more commonly a complication or outcome of rheumatic disease rather than an initiating cause. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships helps clinicians understand how rheumatic fever, chronic rheumatic heart disease, and associated circulatory disorders are interrelated, guiding diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are each described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of abrupt presentation and brief disease course is reflected in the Arabic terminology الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُور الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد, which denotes acute rheumatic inflammation, and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد, which refers to an unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease. Recognizing that these acute rheumatic manifestations share this rapid and limited clinical trajectory aids clinicians in timely diagnosis and appropriate management of rheumatic heart conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is a structural disorder of the heart that is classified as a heart valve disorder; heart valve disorders are located at the mitrální chlopeň (mitral valve). Consequently, rheumatic mitral valve obstruction specifically represents a rheumatic disease‑induced obstruction of the mitral valve, linking it both to the broader category of structural heart disorders and to the subset of disorders affecting the mitral valve.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2513
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart valve stenosis and rheumatic mitral valve obstruction are specific rheumatic conditions that affect the cardiac valves. Both entities are classified within the broader category of heart valve disorders, indicating that they represent pathological processes involving valve function. In addition, rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is identified as a structural disorder of the heart, reflecting that it entails anatomical alteration of the mitral valve apparatus. Together, these relationships illustrate that rheumatic valve disease can present as stenosis or obstruction, and that such manifestations are encompassed by the general concepts of heart valve disorders and structural heart disease, which is important for accurate diagnosis, classification, and management in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 176
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), indicating that patients with a history of rheumatic fever may require these specific valve interventions. Heart valve operations (عمليات صمام القلب) constitute a broad procedural family that includes cardiac valvotomy, excision of heart valve (part of “HEART: EXCISIONS”), valve excision techniques, heart valve repair (رأب الصمام القلبي), and other operations on valves of the heart (Other operations on valves of the heart). Narrower procedures within this category are tricuspid valve replacement with cardiopulmonary bypass (Replacement, tricuspid valve, with cardiopulmonary bypass), transcatheter aortic valve implantation (زراعة الصمام الأبهري عبر القسطرة), pulmonary valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), tricuspid valve repair (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, acute rheumatic myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد), acute rheumatic pericarditis (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد), and acute rheumatic endocarditis (الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ) all share a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a common feature of the various acute rheumatic heart disease manifestations, indicating that clinicians should anticipate an abrupt, short‑lived disease course when evaluating and managing these conditions.
